title: Malaria
oneLiner: Mosquito-borne parasitic infection (usually *P. falciparum*) in returning travellers; a medical emergency if severe — high mortality if missed.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Cerebral malaria:** impaired consciousness (GCS <11, seizures, or focal neurology) in a febrile traveller — requires immediate IV artesunate, not a CT head.
- **Hypoglycaemia:** known complication (especially in children, pregnant women, quinine use) — always check bedside glucose in any febrile traveller with altered behaviour.
- **Severe anaemia / respiratory failure:** extreme pallor + tachypnoea + hypoxia — consider exchange transfusion only after discussion with senior/ICU.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Fever (often cyclical — but can be irregular)
  - Headache, myalgia, arthralgia (flu-like prodrome)
  - Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea (can mimic gastroenteritis)
  - History of recent travel (any duration, including within 3 months) to endemic area
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Pyrexia (may be absent early or with antipyretics)
  - Pallor (anaemia)
  - Jaundice (haemolysis)
  - Splenomegaly (may be tender)
  - Altered consciousness / confusion (cerebral malaria)
  - Petechiae (thrombocytopenia) — not common, but if present, check for disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- Most likely → less likely:
  - **Dengue fever:** fever + severe myalgia + rash + thrombocytopenia — differentiate with RDT (non-specific) and serology (dengue NS1 or IgM)
  - **Typhoid fever:** fever + abdominal pain + rose spots + relative bradycardia — blood culture confirms
  - **Viral hepatitis:** jaundice + fever — LFTs show transaminitis; HAV/HEV IgM
  - **Leptospirosis:** jaundice + conjunctival suffusion + calf tenderness — urine/serology
  - **COVID-19 / influenza:** respiratory symptoms, negative malaria films, positive PCR
  - **Bacterial sepsis:** focus on source (chest, urine, abdomen); blood cultures + procalcitonin
- **Splitter:** Rapid diagnostic test (RDT) positive for *P. falciparum* antigens = malaria (dengue RDT negative). If RDT negative but high suspicion → proceed to thick film.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Immediate rapid diagnostic test (RDT) for malaria (detects *PfHRP2* and pan *pLDH*) — positive = start treatment, do not wait for film
  - Blood glucose (fingerstick) — rule out hypoglycaemia
  - Pulse oximetry / respiratory rate
  - NEWS2 chart
- **Bloods:**
  - Thick and thin films (Giemsa stain) — insert type, quantify parasitaemia (%) and species. Repeat every 6–12 h if initial films negative but clinical suspicion remains high
  - FBC — look for thrombocytopenia, anaemia, neutropaenia
  - U&E, LFT, coagulation screen — assess renal/hepatic function, DIC
  - Blood cultures — before antibiotics if septic
  - G6PD status (quantitative) — essential before primaquine (for *P. vivax/ovale*)
  - Lactate (venous or arterial) — marker of severity
  - HIV serology (consider in severe or recurrent malaria)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: CXR if respiratory symptoms (pulmonary oedema, aspiration)
  - Second-line: CT head if persisting impaired consciousness without clear response to antimalarials (exclude other cause)
- **Special tests:**
  - If *P. vivax* or *P. ovale* suspected → species confirmation (PCR if available) before using primaquine
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - WHO criteria for severe malaria: any of the following → impaired consciousness, respiratory distress, seizures, circulatory collapse, jaundice + organ dysfunction, severe anaemia (Hb <80 g/L in adults), renal impairment (creatinine >265 μmol/L), hypoglycaemia (<2.2 mmol/L), acidosis (bicarbonate <15), lactate >5 mmol/L, parasitaemia >10%
- **Never-miss test:** Thick and thin malaria films in any febrile returning traveller (repeat if negative). Omission = risk of missing malaria until too late.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Severe malaria (any WHO criterion present):
    - IV access, start 0.9% sodium chloride 250 mL bolus if hypotensive (avoid fluid overload in adults)
    - IV artesunate (dose per local policy — see Drugs & Doses)
    - Monitor blood glucose hourly (co-administration with IV artesunate can unmask hypoglycaemia)
    - Paracetamol if fever >38.5°C
    - Consider blood transfusion if Hb <80 g/L with signs of decompensation
  - Uncomplicated malaria (no severe criteria):
    - Oral therapy as soon as tolerated (artemether-lumefantrine or atovaquone-proguanil)
    - Admit for completion of course and monitoring
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Continue oral or IV therapy
  - Repeat thick film every 12–24 h until negative (parasite clearance)
  - Monitor: NEWS2, urine output, daily FBC + U&E + lactate
  - Manage complications as they arise: renal replacement therapy (AKI), ventilation (ARDS), antiepileptics (seizures)
  - Notify local health protection unit (malaria is a notifiable disease)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - For *P. vivax* or *P. ovale* (hypnozoite stage): add primaquine after confirming G6PD > normal (usually 0.25–0.5 mg/kg daily for 14 days, dose per BNF/guidelines — check local policy)
  - Advise avoidance of further mosquito bites / chemoprophylaxis for future travel
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: maintenance IV crystalloids (1–2 L/24 h in adults, titrate to urine output >0.5 mL/kg/h); avoid overhydration in severe malaria.
  - VTE prophylaxis: unless bleeding or DIC (use LMWH after haemostasis confirmed).
  - Diet: light, encourage oral intake; if vomiting → IV fluids + antiemetics.
  - Observations: NEWS2 baseline then at least 4-hourly; more frequent if severe (continuous monitoring if GCS <15).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Severe falciparum malaria (all ages):** IV artesunate — dose per local protocol. [Check local policy.] Common UK regimen: 2.4 mg/kg IV at 0, 12, 24, 48 h, then daily until oral therapy possible. Must be followed by full course of oral artemether-lumefantrine (Riamet).
  - **Uncomplicated falciparum malaria:** Oral artemether-lumefantrine (Riamet) — [check local policy for dose; standard: 4 tablets twice daily for 3 days (weight-based for children)].
    - Alternative: oral atovaquone-proguanil (Malarone) — [check local policy; typically 4 tablets once daily for 3 days].
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Quinine dihydrochloride IV (if artesunate unavailable) + doxycycline or clindamycin (IV then oral) — [check local policy for doses and monitoring].
  - For *P. vivax / ovale*: oral chloroquine (if sensitive) then primaquine after G6PD check.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Primaquine — contraindicated in G6PD deficiency (risk of severe haemolysis). Use only after quantitative G6PD result > normal range.
  - Atovaquone-proguanil — avoid if G6PD deficiency (proguanil can cause haemolysis).
  - Quinine — narrow therapeutic window; can cause hypoglycaemia, arrhythmias (QT prolongation), cinchonism.
  - Antimalarials and antiemetics — check QTc (ondansetron class effect).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Artesunate (IV): no adjustment needed for renal impairment.
  - Primaquine: dose reduction in severe hepatic impairment (avoid if cirrhosis). [Check local policy.]
  - Atovaquone-proguanil: dose reduction in moderate–severe renal impairment (CrCl <30 mL/min — avoid or reduce dose – [check local policy]).
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **IV artesunate — dosing schedule confusion (wrong interval):** Administer at 0, 12, 24, 48 h (not q12h indefinitely). Missing the 12 h dose in severe malaria can delay parasite clearance.
  - **Primaquine without G6PD result:** Can cause massive life-threatening haemolysis. Always wait for G6PD level (quantitative) before prescribing.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Cerebral malaria (seizures, coma, brainstem involvement)
  - Hypoglycaemia (especially children, pregnant women, quinine use)
  - Severe anaemia (Hb <70 g/L)
  - Acute kidney injury (pre-renal / ATN / haemoglobinuria)
  - Pulmonary oedema / ARDS (especially in pregnancy)
  - Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)
  - Acidosis (lactic)
  - Splenic rupture (rare but catastrophic — sudden left upper quadrant pain + shock)
  - Blackwater fever (massive intravascular haemolysis + haemoglobinuria)
- **Chronic:**
  - Post‑malaria neurological sequelae (most recover fully, but residual deficits possible)
  - Nephrotic syndrome (quartan malaria — *P. malariae*)
  - Splenomegaly (tropical splenomegaly syndrome)
  - Recurrent episodes (if hypnozoite stage not treated: *P. vivax/ovale*)
sections.Prognosis:
- **Severe falciparum malaria:** mortality 10–30% even with treatment; higher if multi‑organ failure, late presentation, or pregnancy. Early recognition and prompt IV artesunate reduce deaths.
- **Uncomplicated malaria:** excellent prognosis if treated appropriately. Full recovery within 1–2 weeks. Relapse can occur if *P. vivax/ovale* hypnozoites not cleared.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always repeat thick films every 12–24 h in confirmed malaria** until two consecutive negative films (parasite clearance). Document parasitaemia percentage trend.
- **Check Hb and glucose daily** in all malaria inpatients — hypoglycaemia and anaemia can develop rapidly, even after treatment starts.
- **Notify local health protection unit** for all confirmed malaria cases (public health surveillance + possible source investigation).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not rely on a single negative RDT or thin film** to rule out malaria — repeat thick films at least 2–3 times over 48 h if clinical suspicion remains high (low parasitaemia can be missed).
- **Do not prescribe primaquine without a quantitative G6PD result** — this is a never-event that can cause acute haemolytic crisis.
- **Do not assume initial improvement rules out severe malaria** — survivors of cerebral malaria can deteriorate from secondary infections (e.g., aspiration pneumonia) or hypoglycaemia.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** fever recurs (incomplete clearance), new confusion or drowsiness, jaundice, dark urine, reduced urine output, or bruising/bleeding.
- **Follow-up:** GP or infectious diseases clinic within 72 h of discharge to review parasite clearance films and ensure completion of oral therapy (if switching). Patients treated for *P. vivax/ovale* need repeat G6PD check before primaquine and compliance with 14-day course.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **When to call senior/ICU:**
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 (or any single red parameter)
  - GCS < 15 (especially drop >2 points)
  - Seizure (any)
  - Lactate > 4 mmol/L
  - Hypoxia (SpO2 <92% on room air) or respiratory rate >30/min
  - Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for 2 consecutive hours
  - Parasitaemia >10% or rising despite IV therapy
  - Systolic BP <90 mmHg (or MAP <65) not responding to fluid bolus
  - Any WHO severe malaria criterion newly met
- **Crash team criteria:** Cardiac arrest, GCS <8 (consider securing airway), unresponsive hypoglycaemia, airway compromise.

rawMarkdown:
## Malaria

**One-liner:** Mosquito-borne parasitic infection (usually *P. falciparum*) in returning travellers; a medical emergency if severe — high mortality if missed.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Cerebral malaria:** impaired consciousness (GCS <11, seizures, or focal neurology) in a febrile traveller — requires immediate IV artesunate, not a CT head.
- **Hypoglycaemia:** known complication (especially in children, pregnant women, quinine use) — always check bedside glucose in any febrile traveller with altered behaviour.
- **Severe anaemia / respiratory failure:** extreme pallor + tachypnoea + hypoxia — consider exchange transfusion only after discussion with senior/ICU.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Fever (often cyclical — but can be irregular)
  - Headache, myalgia, arthralgia (flu-like prodrome)
  - Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea (can mimic gastroenteritis)
  - History of recent travel (any duration, including within 3 months) to endemic area
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Pyrexia (may be absent early or with antipyretics)
  - Pallor (anaemia)
  - Jaundice (haemolysis)
  - Splenomegaly (may be tender)
  - Altered consciousness / confusion (cerebral malaria)
  - Petechiae (thrombocytopenia) — not common, but if present, check for disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)

### Differential Diagnosis
- Most likely → less likely:
  - **Dengue fever:** fever + severe myalgia + rash + thrombocytopenia — differentiate with RDT (non-specific) and serology (dengue NS1 or IgM)
  - **Typhoid fever:** fever + abdominal pain + rose spots + relative bradycardia — blood culture confirms
  - **Viral hepatitis:** jaundice + fever — LFTs show transaminitis; HAV/HEV IgM
  - **Leptospirosis:** jaundice + conjunctival suffusion + calf tenderness — urine/serology
  - **COVID-19 / influenza:** respiratory symptoms, negative malaria films, positive PCR
  - **Bacterial sepsis:** focus on source (chest, urine, abdomen); blood cultures + procalcitonin
- **Splitter:** Rapid diagnostic test (RDT) positive for *P. falciparum* antigens = malaria (dengue RDT negative). If RDT negative but high suspicion → proceed to thick film.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Immediate rapid diagnostic test (RDT) for malaria (detects *PfHRP2* and pan *pLDH*) — positive = start treatment, do not wait for film
  - Blood glucose (fingerstick) — rule out hypoglycaemia
  - Pulse oximetry / respiratory rate
  - NEWS2 chart
- **Bloods:**
  - Thick and thin films (Giemsa stain) — insert type, quantify parasitaemia (%) and species. Repeat every 6–12 h if initial films negative but clinical suspicion remains high
  - FBC — look for thrombocytopenia, anaemia, neutropaenia
  - U&E, LFT, coagulation screen — assess renal/hepatic function, DIC
  - Blood cultures — before antibiotics if septic
  - G6PD status (quantitative) — essential before primaquine (for *P. vivax/ovale*)
  - Lactate (venous or arterial) — marker of severity
  - HIV serology (consider in severe or recurrent malaria)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: CXR if respiratory symptoms (pulmonary oedema, aspiration)
  - Second-line: CT head if persisting impaired consciousness without clear response to antimalarials (exclude other cause)
- **Special tests:**
  - If *P. vivax* or *P. ovale* suspected → species confirmation (PCR if available) before using primaquine
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - WHO criteria for severe malaria: any of the following → impaired consciousness, respiratory distress, seizures, circulatory collapse, jaundice + organ dysfunction, severe anaemia (Hb <80 g/L in adults), renal impairment (creatinine >265 μmol/L), hypoglycaemia (<2.2 mmol/L), acidosis (bicarbonate <15), lactate >5 mmol/L, parasitaemia >10%
- **Never-miss test:** Thick and thin malaria films in any febrile returning traveller (repeat if negative). Omission = risk of missing malaria until too late.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Severe malaria (any WHO criterion present):
    - IV access, start 0.9% sodium chloride 250 mL bolus if hypotensive (avoid fluid overload in adults)
    - IV artesunate (dose per local policy — see Drugs & Doses)
    - Monitor blood glucose hourly (co-administration with IV artesunate can unmask hypoglycaemia)
    - Paracetamol if fever >38.5°C
    - Consider blood transfusion if Hb <80 g/L with signs of decompensation
  - Uncomplicated malaria (no severe criteria):
    - Oral therapy as soon as tolerated (artemether-lumefantrine or atovaquone-proguanil)
    - Admit for completion of course and monitoring
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Continue oral or IV therapy
  - Repeat thick film every 12–24 h until negative (parasite clearance)
  - Monitor: NEWS2, urine output, daily FBC + U&E + lactate
  - Manage complications as they arise: renal replacement therapy (AKI), ventilation (ARDS), antiepileptics (seizures)
  - Notify local health protection unit (malaria is a notifiable disease)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - For *P. vivax* or *P. ovale* (hypnozoite stage): add primaquine after confirming G6PD > normal (usually 0.25–0.5 mg/kg daily for 14 days, dose per BNF/guidelines — check local policy)
  - Advise avoidance of further mosquito bites / chemoprophylaxis for future travel
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: maintenance IV crystalloids (1–2 L/24 h in adults, titrate to urine output >0.5 mL/kg/h); avoid overhydration in severe malaria.
  - VTE prophylaxis: unless bleeding or DIC (use LMWH after haemostasis confirmed).
  - Diet: light, encourage oral intake; if vomiting → IV fluids + antiemetics.
  - Observations: NEWS2 baseline then at least 4-hourly; more frequent if severe (continuous monitoring if GCS <15).

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Severe falciparum malaria (all ages):** IV artesunate — dose per local protocol. [Check local policy.] Common UK regimen: 2.4 mg/kg IV at 0, 12, 24, 48 h, then daily until oral therapy possible. Must be followed by full course of oral artemether-lumefantrine (Riamet).
  - **Uncomplicated falciparum malaria:** Oral artemether-lumefantrine (Riamet) — [check local policy for dose; standard: 4 tablets twice daily for 3 days (weight-based for children)].
    - Alternative: oral atovaquone-proguanil (Malarone) — [check local policy; typically 4 tablets once daily for 3 days].
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Quinine dihydrochloride IV (if artesunate unavailable) + doxycycline or clindamycin (IV then oral) — [check local policy for doses and monitoring].
  - For *P. vivax / ovale*: oral chloroquine (if sensitive) then primaquine after G6PD check.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Primaquine — contraindicated in G6PD deficiency (risk of severe haemolysis). Use only after quantitative G6PD result > normal range.
  - Atovaquone-proguanil — avoid if G6PD deficiency (proguanil can cause haemolysis).
  - Quinine — narrow therapeutic window; can cause hypoglycaemia, arrhythmias (QT prolongation), cinchonism.
  - Antimalarials and antiemetics — check QTc (ondansetron class effect).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Artesunate (IV): no adjustment needed for renal impairment.
  - Primaquine: dose reduction in severe hepatic impairment (avoid if cirrhosis). [Check local policy.]
  - Atovaquone-proguanil: dose reduction in moderate–severe renal impairment (CrCl <30 mL/min — avoid or reduce dose – [check local policy]).
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **IV artesunate — dosing schedule confusion (wrong interval):** Administer at 0, 12, 24, 48 h (not q12h indefinitely). Missing the 12 h dose in severe malaria can delay parasite clearance.
  - **Primaquine without G6PD result:** Can cause massive life-threatening haemolysis. Always wait for G6PD level (quantitative) before prescribing.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Cerebral malaria (seizures, coma, brainstem involvement)
  - Hypoglycaemia (especially children, pregnant women, quinine use)
  - Severe anaemia (Hb <70 g/L)
  - Acute kidney injury (pre-renal / ATN / haemoglobinuria)
  - Pulmonary oedema / ARDS (especially in pregnancy)
  - Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)
  - Acidosis (lactic)
  - Splenic rupture (rare but catastrophic — sudden left upper quadrant pain + shock)
  - Blackwater fever (massive intravascular haemolysis + haemoglobinuria)
- **Chronic:**
  - Post‑malaria neurological sequelae (most recover fully, but residual deficits possible)
  - Nephrotic syndrome (quartan malaria — *P. malariae*)
  - Splenomegaly (tropical splenomegaly syndrome)
  - Recurrent episodes (if hypnozoite stage not treated: *P. vivax/ovale*)

### Prognosis
- **Severe falciparum malaria:** mortality 10–30% even with treatment; higher if multi‑organ failure, late presentation, or pregnancy. Early recognition and prompt IV artesunate reduce deaths.
- **Uncomplicated malaria:** excellent prognosis if treated appropriately. Full recovery within 1–2 weeks. Relapse can occur if *P. vivax/ovale* hypnozoites not cleared.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always repeat thick films every 12–24 h in confirmed malaria** until two consecutive negative films (parasite clearance). Document parasitaemia percentage trend.
- **Check Hb and glucose daily** in all malaria inpatients — hypoglycaemia and anaemia can develop rapidly, even after treatment starts.
- **Notify local health protection unit** for all confirmed malaria cases (public health surveillance + possible source investigation).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not rely on a single negative RDT or thin film** to rule out malaria — repeat thick films at least 2–3 times over 48 h if clinical suspicion remains high (low parasitaemia can be missed).
- **Do not prescribe primaquine without a quantitative G6PD result** — this is a never-event that can cause acute haemolytic crisis.
- **Do not assume initial improvement rules out severe malaria** — survivors of cerebral malaria can deteriorate from secondary infections (e.g., aspiration pneumonia) or hypoglycaemia.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** fever recurs (incomplete clearance), new confusion or drowsiness, jaundice, dark urine, reduced urine output, or bruising/bleeding.
- **Follow-up:** GP or infectious diseases clinic within 72 h of discharge to review parasite clearance films and ensure completion of oral therapy (if switching). Patients treated for *P. vivax/ovale* need repeat G6PD check before primaquine and compliance with 14-day course.

### Escalation Criteria
- **When to call senior/ICU:**
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 (or any single red parameter)
  - GCS < 15 (especially drop >2 points)
  - Seizure (any)
  - Lactate > 4 mmol/L
  - Hypoxia (SpO2 <92% on room air) or respiratory rate >30/min
  - Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for 2 consecutive hours
  - Parasitaemia >10% or rising despite IV therapy
  - Systolic BP <90 mmHg (or MAP <65) not responding to fluid bolus
  - Any WHO severe malaria criterion newly met
- **Crash team criteria:** Cardiac arrest, GCS <8 (consider securing airway), unresponsive hypoglycaemia, airway compromise.
